title: How to Use Contemplative Practices for Financial Abundance
body:
Your Money Story is a Lie - Here's How to Rewrite It

Truth is, you're broke, or you feel broke, or you're terrified of being broke, not because you're bad with numbers, but because your money story is a tangled mess of inherited fear, societal pressure, and unprocessed pain. It's not about your budget, not really. It's about what you believe you deserve, what you believe is possible, and the invisible threads of trauma that pull you back every time you try to step forward. You feel that? That knot in your stomach when you check your bank account? That's not just stress about bills. That's your nervous system reacting to a lifetime of scarcity programming. It's deep. It's pervasive. And it's keeping you from the abundance that's already waiting for you.

We're told to hustle harder, save more, invest smarter. All good advice, don't get me wrong. Ramit Sethi's I Will Teach You to Be Rich (paid link) is a phenomenal resource for practical money management. But you can follow every rule, track every penny, and still feel like you're fighting an uphill battle if you haven't addressed the root cause. The poverty consciousness doesn't care how much you earn. It'll find a way to keep you feeling small, insecure, and perpetually behind, even with a six-figure salary. This isn't about blaming you; it's about helping you see the invisible prison you're in. This is about breaking free.

The Invisible Chains: Unpacking Your Money Wound

Here's the thing, your relationship with money isn't logical. It's emotional. It's spiritual. It's a mirror reflecting your deepest beliefs about worth, safety, and belonging. Think about it. What were the messages you received about money growing up? Was it a source of stress? A secret? A tool for control? Were you told money doesn't grow on trees? That rich people are greedy? That you'll always struggle just like your parents did?

These aren't just quaint sayings. They're deeply ingrained programs running in your subconscious, shaping every financial decision you make, often without you even knowing it. This is what I mean when I say the money wound runs deeper than your budget spreadsheet. It's in your bones, in your breath, in your automatic reactions. It's why you might spend impulsively when you feel stressed, or hoard every penny out of fear, or sabotage opportunities for growth because deep down, you don't feel worthy of more. Geneen Roth, with her work on emotional eating and money, shows us how deeply intertwined our emotional lives are with our relationship to resources. It's never just about the food, or just about the money. It's about the feelings we're trying to manage, avoid, or express.

Contemplative practices aren't about magic. They're about awareness. They're about shining a light into those dark corners of your subconscious where these old stories reside. They're about creating space between the trigger and your automatic reaction. They're about seeing the stories for what they are - just stories - and choosing to write new ones. Money is just energy with a story attached to it. What story are you attaching to yours?

Meditation: Your Gateway to Financial Clarity

You might be thinking, "Meditate to make more money? Kalesh, you've lost it." But hear me out. Meditation isn't about sitting cross-legged chanting "Om" (though it can be). It's about training your attention. It's about observing your thoughts and feelings without judgment. And when it comes to money, that's revolutionary.

Start small. Five minutes a day. Find a quiet spot. Close your eyes. Just notice your breath. When your mind wanders (and it will, relentlessly), gently bring it back to your breath. That's it. No need to clear your mind. Just observe. What happens when you think about your debt? What sensations arise in your body? What thoughts pop up about your income? Don't try to fix them. Don't judge them. Just notice. This simple act of observation creates distance. It allows you to see your thoughts and feelings as separate from you, not as who you are.

As you get more comfortable, you can direct your meditation specifically to money. Visualize abundance. Not just a pile of cash, but the feeling of security, of freedom, of generosity that money can bring. See yourself making wise decisions. See yourself contributing to causes you care about. See yourself experiencing joy without the constant hum of financial anxiety. Sadhguru often speaks about the power of inner engineering - aligning your inner world with your outer aspirations. Meditation is the first step in that engineering process.

This isn't about "manifesting" in some airy-fairy way. It's about reprogramming your subconscious. When you consistently visualize and feel abundance, you start to shift your internal state. You become more open to opportunities. You make decisions from a place of possibility, not scarcity. Right?! Your internal world starts to align with the external reality you want to create.

Journaling: Excavating Hidden Beliefs

Your journal isn't just a place for daily musings. It's a direct line to your subconscious. It's where you can unearth those hidden money beliefs that are silently running the show. Barbara Huson, in her book Sacred Success, really emphasizes the importance of understanding your financial psychology. Journaling is a powerful tool for that.

Here are some prompts to get you started:

  • What's my earliest memory of money? How did it feel?
  • What did my parents/guardians teach me about money, explicitly and implicitly?
  • What's my biggest fear about money? What's the worst-case scenario?
  • If I had all the money I ever needed, what would I do? What would I feel?
  • What do I believe about rich people? About poor people?
  • Where do I feel financially stuck right now? What belief is holding me there?
  • What's the most common financial advice I ignore? Why?

Don't edit yourself. Just write. Let it flow. You'll be amazed at what comes up. You'll see patterns. You'll recognize old stories that aren't even yours. You might discover that the reason you can't seem to save is because you subconsciously believe that having money makes you a target, or that it's "not spiritual." You might find that you're constantly overspending because you're trying to fill an emotional void. Feelings Buried Alive Never Die (paid link) by Karol K. Truman is a fantastic resource for understanding how unaddressed emotions impact every area of your life, including your finances.

Once you see these beliefs, you can start to challenge them. Are they true? Are they serving you? If not, what new belief do you want to adopt? Journaling isn't just about discovery; it's about intentional reprogramming. It's about consciously choosing the story you want your money to tell. Know what I mean?

Mindful Spending and Earning: Conscious Exchange

Most of us spend money on autopilot. We swipe, we click, we barely register the exchange. But what if every financial transaction became an opportunity for mindfulness? What if you brought intention to every dollar you spent and every dollar you earned?

Before you buy something, pause. Ask yourself: Do I truly need this? Is this aligned with my values? Am I buying this out of genuine desire or out of fear, boredom, or a need to impress? This isn't about deprivation. It's about conscious consumption. It's about honoring your resources and making choices that genuinely serve you, not just fleeting impulses.

And on the earning side, how do you feel about the money you receive? Do you feel grateful? Or do you feel guilty, unworthy, or like it's never enough? Bari Tessler, with her work in "The Art of Money," really emphasizes this whole-person approach - integrating your inner world with your outer financial practices. When you receive money, whether it's a paycheck, a gift, or a found dollar, pause. Acknowledge it. Thank it. Feel the energy of it. This might sound woo-woo, but it shifts your energetic relationship with money from one of scarcity and struggle to one of appreciation and flow.

Try this: For one week, before every purchase, take three deep breaths. Ask yourself, "Is this a 'hell yes'?" If it's not, reconsider. And when money comes in, spend a minute acknowledging it. Feel the gratitude. This simple practice will dramatically change your relationship with your finances. It's about recognizing that money isn't just a number; it's a flow of energy, and you're an active participant in that flow.

Gratitude: The Amplifier of Abundance

Gratitude isn't just a nice feeling; it's a powerful energetic state. When you're in a state of gratitude, you're signaling to the universe (and your subconscious) that you have enough. That you are enough. This is a complete reversal of the scarcity mindset, which constantly focuses on what's lacking.

Start a daily gratitude practice specifically for money. It doesn't have to be about millions. Be grateful for the roof over your head, the food in your fridge, the device you're reading this on. Be grateful for the money you *do* have, no matter how small. Be grateful for the opportunities to earn. Be grateful for your ability to learn and grow. Even if you're struggling, find one thing to be grateful for related to money. Maybe it's that you have a job, even if it's not your dream job. Maybe it's that you have access to clean water. Maybe it's that you have friends who support you. These are all forms of abundance, even if they don't appear on your bank statement.

Sadhguru often talks about the importance of being in a constant state of pleasantness. Gratitude is a direct path to that. When you feel grateful, you open yourself up to receiving more. It's not magic, it's psychology. When you're grateful, you're more optimistic, more resilient, and more likely to spot opportunities that you might otherwise miss when you're caught in a cycle of fear and lack.

Does that land? Seriously. Try it for a week. Every morning, list 5 things you're grateful for about your financial situation, no matter how small or seemingly insignificant. Watch what shifts.

Self-Compassion: Healing the Shame of Scarcity

Let's be honest. Money shame is a real thing. It's that awful feeling that you're not good enough, not smart enough, not doing enough, because of your financial situation. It makes you hide, isolate, and often leads to self-sabotage. You can't heal what you hate, and if you're constantly judging yourself for your financial past or present, you're just perpetuating the cycle of struggle.

Contemplative practices, especially those rooted in self-compassion, are crucial here. Kristin Neff's work on self-compassion is incredibly valuable. Treat yourself like you would a dear friend who is struggling with money. Would you berate them? Call them names? Tell them they're a failure? Of course not. You'd offer understanding, kindness, and support. Extend that same kindness to yourself.

When you catch yourself in a spiral of negative money thoughts, pause. Place a hand over your heart. Say to yourself, "This is a moment of financial struggle. Struggle is a part of life. May I be kind to myself in this moment." This isn't about excusing irresponsible behavior. It's about creating a safe space within yourself to address the issues without the added burden of shame. Shame paralyzes. Compassion motivates.

Remember, the money wound runs deep. It's often intertwined with feelings of unworthiness that have nothing to do with your actual financial decisions. It's a historical wound. Be patient with yourself. This isn't a quick fix. It's a journey of deep healing and conscious creation. You're unlearning decades of conditioning. That takes time, effort, and a whole lot of self-love.

Giving: The Ultimate Act of Abundance

This might seem counterintuitive when you're struggling with money, but giving, even a small amount, is one of the most powerful ways to shift your money mindset. It's an act of faith. It's a declaration to yourself and the universe that you have enough to share. It breaks the cycle of scarcity by demonstrating generosity.

It doesn't have to be a huge sum. Even a few dollars to a charity you believe in, or buying a coffee for someone, or volunteering your time - these are all acts of giving. The key is the intention behind it. Give from a place of genuine generosity and gratitude, not from a place of obligation or fear. The Soul of Money (paid link) by Lynne Twist talks extensively about how money can be a source for good and how our relationship with it can be transformed through conscious giving.

When you give, you experience the joy of contribution. You feel connected. You experience abundance not just as something you receive, but as something you create and share. This shifts your identity from someone who is always lacking to someone who is capable of giving. It's a real change that ripples through every area of your life, including your financial well-being.

So, if you're feeling stuck, try this: find a cause you care about and give even a dollar. Or volunteer an hour of your time. Notice how it feels. Notice the shift in your energy. This isn't about sacrificing what you don't have. It's about demonstrating the abundance you already possess within you.

FAQs About Contemplative Practices and Money

How quickly will I see results from these practices?

Honestly, it varies. Some people feel shifts almost immediately - a reduction in anxiety, a new insight, a sense of hope. For others, it's a slower, more gradual process. This isn't a get-rich-quick scheme. It's about deep, internal change. The "results" aren't just about your bank account balance (though that often improves). They're about your peace of mind, your sense of security, and your overall well-being. Stick with it. Consistency is key.

Do I need to be spiritual to do this?

Absolutely not. While these practices have roots in spiritual traditions, you don't need to adopt any particular belief system. Think of them as mental and emotional training. You're learning to observe your mind, understand your emotions, and intentionally direct your focus. It's psychology, neuroscience, and self-awareness all rolled into one. If you're open to exploring your inner world and willing to try something different, you're all set.

What if I don't have time for all of this?

I hear you. Life is busy. But you have time for what you prioritize. Start small. Even five minutes of meditation a day. Five minutes of journaling. A conscious breath before a purchase. These aren't huge time commitments. The goal isn't perfection, it's consistency. Small, daily practices compound over time and create massive shifts. Don't let the idea of "all of this" overwhelm you. Pick one thing and commit to it for a week. See how it feels. You'll likely find that these practices actually save you time and energy in the long run by reducing stress and improving clarity.

Your financial reality is a reflection of your inner world. If you want to change your outer world, you have to tend to your inner one. No amount of budgeting or saving will truly free you if you're still operating from a place of deep-seated fear and unworthiness. These contemplative practices aren't just about getting more money; they're about becoming a person who is capable of receiving, managing, and enjoying true abundance, without the constant anxiety and struggle. They're about healing your relationship with yourself, which then naturally extends to your relationship with money. It's time to shed those old stories. It's time to reclaim your power.

Your challenge today: take five minutes. Just five. Close your eyes. Notice your breath. And then, without judgment, bring your attention to your financial situation. What thoughts come up? What feelings? Just observe. That's it. That's the first step to rewriting your money story.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Discover how contemplative practices can transform your relationship with money. Rewrite your money story, overcome financial fears, and cultivate abundance.
author: Kalesh
